data.title: RSP: Why I Am Upgrading This Large-Cap ETF
data.description:
One of my concerns with stocks after a three-year run fueled by the performance of the Magnificent 7 or the Elite 8 was the high concentration of the S&P 500 in just a few stocks. At the end of November, I wrote about the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), calling it a unique and compelling ETF but initiating it with a Hold. Since that article, RSP has outpaced the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY):

The idea to Hold RSP instead of SPY was working very well in Q1, when RSP returned 0.6% as SPY returned -4.4%, but Q2 has seen SPY outperform RSP:

Today, though the price is higher, I am upgrading RSP to Buy. In this article, I explain again the ETF and discuss why I am upgrading it.

An Update on the $87.2 Billion ETF

At the end of November, when I first wrote about RSP, it had $75.2 billion in assets. It has increased by 16% to $87.2 billion. The price is up 5.5%, but the majority of the increase is due to inflows that resulted in share creation. Yes, equal-weight, the methodology of this ETF, has gained popularity in 2026 relative to market-cap weighting, such as SPY or the larger Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) use.

$87 billion is big, but it is much smaller than the assets under management for SPY at $725 billion or VOO at $909 billion. RSP is widely followed, with 22,580 Seeking Alpha followers compared to 20,000 in November (+13%), but SPY (597K vs. 572K, up 4%) and VOO (198K vs. 187K then, up 6%) are much more widely followed.

Invesco (IVZ), which launched RSP in 2003, updates its website for the ETF regularly, and the RSP Fact Sheet, last published as of March 31st, helps understand what the ETF is. Quite simply, RSP tracks an index that is based upon the S&P 500 and updates and rebalances quarterly. The ETF has a management fee of 0.2%, which is quite low compared to most ETFs but certainly higher than SPY (0.0945%) or VOO (0.03%).

The 10 largest stocks in RSP make up just 3% of the ETF currently, while the SPY top 10 comprise 38.3% (as of April 23rd). RSP has the exact same stocks, but the weightings are different in the stocks. The sector weightings are different too. At the beginning of the month, RSP's sector allocations were more equally weighted than the S&P 500:

S&P 500 weightings were a lot heavier on Technology:

The S&P 500 has more than twice as much exposure to Technology than the equal-weighted index. Other large differences (greater than 2%) between the weightings include higher exposures of RSP as of the end of Q1 in Industrials (+6.5%), Real Estate (+4.1%), Utilities (+3.8%), Materials (+3.3%), Financials (+2.9%), and Health Care (+2.1%) as well as lower exposures in Communications Services (6.4%). The S&P 500 has eleven GICS, and the weightings in two are substantially lower for RSP and substantially higher for six.

One of the interesting things about sector performance for the S&P 500 in 2026 is that the largest sectors in the S&P 500 have generally underperformed the smaller ones. Here are the returns year-to-date on the 11 State Street ETFs that track these sectors of the S&P 500 (sorted by weight from largest to smallest):

So, four of the five largest sectors in the S&P 500 are down year-to-date as we approach the end of April. The largest sector is up, which is helping the S&P 500 return about 5% year-to-date. Not all the smaller sectors are outpacing Technology, but three are.

RSP has the highest exposure to Industrials, which are up more than twice as much as the S&P 500, but its second largest, Financials, are trailing badly. Its fourth largest, Health Care, is also performing poorly in the S&P 500 by market-cap weighting.

Kudos to Invesco for offering this equal-weight ETF that is large and increasingly popular. The firm has not had as much success with other equal-weighted ETFs. They do offer ETFs based on GICS, with Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Technology ETF (RSPT) standing out with $4.6 billion in assets under management. I did write about this one three months ago, initiating it with a Hold and calling it a better way to invest in Technology. Since then, it has outpaced State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK), gaining 12.6% compared to the 10.2% advance in XLK. The other equal-weight Sector ETFs are all less than $1 billion in assets. Here are the year-to-date returns for them:

Similar to the market-cap-weighted sectors, there are four equal-weighted sectors that are down in 2026, and they are the same. Interestingly, some of the eleven equal-weighted sector ETFs are doing better than the market-cap-weighted ones, and some are doing worse. Here are the two that are doing better by more than 3% and the three that are doing worse by more than 3%:

I like that the equal-weight Technology companies are outpacing the market-cap-weighted ones and that the very best sector, Energy, has better returns with equal weighting. RSP is seeing its performance get hurt by the performance of Industrials, its largest exposure, relative to SPY and VOO.

Invesco shares metrics for RSP, and the ETF appears to be cheaper than SPY on a PE basis and according to the price-to-book value ratio. Invesco views the P/E ratio as 16.7X as of the end of Q1, which is less than the 22.4X for SPY, according to State Street. The price-to-book value ratio is 3.1X as of the end of March, compared to SPY at 5.2X currently.

RSP Has Underperformed the S&P 500

While RSP is doing better than SPY and VOO in 2026, it has lagged badly since the end of 2022:

Again, this move higher in the S&P 500 was driven by the strong performance of the very largest stocks. Here are the returns of the Elite 8 since the end of 2022:

Only one of the stocks has underperformed SPY, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and it has returned 81.7% even after the rough start to 2026. The other seven have all more than doubled.

For investors who believe that winners keep winning, there are many ETFs that focus on the Magnificent 7, the Elite 8, or the very largest companies. For those that are invested in ETFs or funds that target the S&P 500 and are passive, they are very invested in the Elite 8.

Invesco shares a 10-year chart on the Fact Sheet of the return of RSP compared to the S&P 500 as well as the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index as of the end of Q1:

While the last three years have been terrible for equal weight relative to market-cap weighted, RSP did outperform the S&P 500 in 2022 when the market fell and even outpaced it in the rallying market of 2021 marginally.

Why RSP Can Outpace the S&P 500

Predicting how RSP will perform is challenging. It should track the S&P 500 roughly, but the history shows that it can outpace it in rallying markets or in falling markets, and it can certainly underperform it even in falling markets (like 2018).

2026 is seeing two very strong trends: a preference for smaller companies and very different returns by sector for the S&P 500. April has seen a reversion, with the largest companies rallying and the largest part of the S&P 500, Technology, leading the way. In April, the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) has outpaced VOO and SPY, rallying 11.6%, so this trend continues.

I do like smaller companies generally and have written favorably about two ETFs that I include in my ETF model portfolio, ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers (SMDV) and ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats (REGL), both of which I have rated as Buy. In addition to those two ETFs, I include RSP, which is now slightly larger than both of them.

My model portfolio is a balanced portfolio and tries to outpace the S&P 500 for the equity component. While I think that small caps and mid caps can do better than the S&P 500 going forward, I see less risk in an equal-weighted S&P 500, like RSP. When I think about scenarios going forward for RSP, the three that I am evaluating are:

  1. S&P 500 keeps rallying, with the largest stocks leading the way
  2. S&P 500 falls sharply
  3. S&P 500 doesn't change much, while the trends in place for smaller companies remain in place

The bullish scenario includes presumably inflation decline and AI spending continuing to grow rapidly. The bearish scenario includes interest rates continuing to rise and the economy slowing. In the more neutral scenario, the trends established this year towards smaller stocks being in favor likely continues.

In the first scenario, RSP should lag. Assuming the S&P 500 advances in price by 20% in 2026, the total return would be about 21.0%. The S&P 500 would advance in price by 14.6%, and RSP would probably rally by 11%. This would give RSP a price return of 17.1% for the year and a total return of 18.8%. With this lag in performance, it failed to beat the S&P 500 for the year, losing by about 2%. This would be much better relative performance than the past three years!

In the very bearish scenario, I assume that the S&P 500 will fall back to its lows set in April 2025. This would be a price decline of 32.5%. In this scenario, perhaps RSP would do even better than I project, but I assume RSP would fall 29% to about $143.40, which is below its April 2025 low that was slightly above $150.00. For the full year, the S&P 500 would return -28.5% with dividends included, while the RSP total return would be -20.2%. The first win since 2022 would be an advantage of 8.3% in total return. While this is large, it compares to an underperformance of 12.6% in 2023 and 12.2% in 2024 and is only slightly larger than the underperformance of 6.6% in 2025.

In the neutral scenario, I assume that the S&P 500 ends up staying near where it is now over the balance of the year, which would give a total return for the year of 5.7%. In this scenario, I assume that the very largest stocks do what they were doing in Q1 with the rest of the S&P 500 doing somewhat better. If the top 10 names by market cap were to fall in price by 10%, the other names would rally in total by about 6.2%. If this were to happen, RSP would increase in price from here by about 5.9%.

Using the same weights as I did last week in my REGL upgrade (20% S&P 500 bull, 30% bear, 50% neutral), this works out to an expected price return of -0.65% for RSP. The total return would be 0.50%. This is lower than the return on cash currently, but it looks a lot better than the S&P 500, which would return -3.6% before dividends.

It is hard to get excited by an ETF that I expect in these scenarios will have a probability-weighted total return of just 0.5%. Last week, when I upgraded REGL, my total return using the same weights worked out to be 11%, which exceeds cash and my expected return of the S&P 500. As I have said, though, I am bearish on stocks. RSP, even after this move higher in the market, is still beating SPY and VOO in a rallying market, and I think that there is a lot of room for the trends to continue. The scenarios that I shared were conservative. It is possible that RSP could fall a lot less than I had it falling in my bear scenario, and it could also do a lot better in the neutral scenario. So, I am upgrading RSP to Buy.

Conclusion

I liked RSP enough in late November to write an article on it, and I did include it on my ETF watch list at year-end that now totals 85. I wish I had rated it a Buy five months ago rather than today, and I feel a bit awkward upgrading it at this higher price, especially as I feel even more bearish on stocks today than I did five months ago. I like RSP more than SPY.

When I was conveying my negativity towards stocks in late 2025, I was suggesting cash (and TIPS), but many readers questioned what they were supposed to buy if they wanted or needed to own stocks. I understand this, and I developed a Buy rating for SMDV, which has done very well. I upgraded REGL last week too. I continue to view both of these ETFs as likely to perform better than RSP, but, as I said above, my position size is larger in RSP right now.

Buying RSP makes sense compared to owning SPY or VOO. For investors who are willing to look at smaller market caps, I think that there are better ideas, but RSP should perform better in two of the three scenarios that I discussed, and perhaps I was overly conservative in one or both of those and in my bearish scenario.

data.title: Dodge & Cox International Stock Fund Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Market Commentary

International equity markets declined in the first quarter of 2026, as the conflict in Iran disrupted global energy supply and reversed the rally from the first six weeks of the year. The conflict effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz—a critical transportation corridor for roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas supply—and pushed Brent crude prices above $100 per barrel. The surge in energy prices fueled inflation concerns across both developed and emerging economies.

The quarter unfolded in two distinct phases. Through mid-February, global equity markets rose, with international equities extending their lead over U.S. markets. Within technology, advancements in AI raised concerns about its potential to disrupt industries ranging from software and data providers to commercial real estate and financial services. The escalation of the Iran conflict in late February triggered a broad selloff and wide dispersion at the sector level. In the first quarter, the Energy sector surged 28.5% due to supply disruptions, while more economically sensitive areas declined: Consumer Discretionary fell 13.3% and Communication Services declined 9.5%, as inflation weighed on the economic outlook.

International value stocks continued to outperform growth stocks during the quarter. The MSCI ACWI ex USA Value Index outpaced the MSCI ACWI ex USA Growth Index by almost six percentage points, driven primarily by the Value Index's higher exposure to the Energy sector. 2 Despite this outperformance, the MSCI ACWI ex USA Value trades at 11.6 times forward earnings, a significant discount to its growth counterpart at 17.2 times forward earnings. 3 The trajectory of the conflict, its implications for energy supply, and the potential for sustained higher inflation remain key risks for international equity markets.

Current month-end performance can be obtained at Investment Focus | Dodge & Cox or by calling 800-621-3979.

Portfolio Strategy

During the first quarter, we found several opportunities to invest in high-quality companies that we believe the market has mispriced due to fears of disruption from AI. For example, we added to the Fund's position in Booking Holdings, the largest online travel agency globally, with particularly strong positions in Europe and parts of Asia. 4 While there are concerns in the market that AI platforms, such as ChatGPT, will disintermediate consumer marketplaces like Booking, we believe this underestimates the durability of Booking's market position, supported by the depth and breadth of services they provide to their accommodations suppliers, the stickiness of their traveler customer base, and the operational complexity of the service they provide. Moreover, we think the company has significant opportunities to improve both its products and cost structure with AI.

Elsewhere in the portfolio, we reallocated capital to companies with fee-based business models such as Brookfield, a leading global alternative asset manager focused on real assets, like infrastructure and real estate. We believe Brookfield offers resilient earnings over our investment horizon, supported by strong fundraising momentum, a dedicated AI infrastructure fund that derisks its core franchise, and significant holdings of income-generating assets. Recent weakness among alternative asset managers, driven by concerns of software exposure in private credit, has created an attractive opportunity given Brookfield's real asset focus and minimal exposure to direct lending.

Outlook

Reflecting our value orientation, the Fund trades at 12.4 times forward earnings, a discount to the MSCI ACWI ex USA at 13.7 times. We believe the portfolio is well positioned across a broad range of economic and market environments. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

data.title: Nvidia: Biting The Bullet And Upgrading My Rating
data.description:
Revisiting A Market Titan

Coming up on almost 6 months since I last covered Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), I figured it might be time for a revisit, seeing as this week we have plenty of large tech earnings reports that might influence how NVDA moves or the narrative around it. I was, I think, rightfully critical of NVDA as an investment the last time I covered it, rating it a Hold, but something which has still returned 13.33%, beating the S&P 500's (SP500) 4.04% during the same period.

I remain perhaps more on the bearish side for the short-term as I do think there might be a hit to revenues and that customers like Amazon and Meta might have to reign in on their spending. Competition from Google is also serious as they look to develop their own by themselves.

This was my core argument and concerns back in December. I talked about how revenues might be hit because CapEx spending would eventually have to decline because the companies couldn't keep up the spending. At the core of it, it revolves around the GPUs having a shorter depreciation period than what is reported. Even though these customers have incredible profit margins, throwing $100 billion on buying GPUs every other year would be crippling to their business eventually. This would come straight out of the revenues of NVDA as fewer orders come in.

I was perhaps a bit wary talking about that; hyperscalers actually need to post some ROI; otherwise, these massive investments make little financial sense. I think the market narrative has changed to this as well. But then you have NVDA posting an absolutely stunning earnings report with guidance well above expectations. This again shifts the narrative, and I think it eases any concerns that NVDA’s revenues will slow down. I was still on the fence about what to rate the stock leading up to this article, but I think I’m going to bite the bullet and rate it a Buy now. My concerns are still there, but NVDA seems to be able to tackle this, and the way their product portfolio is developing, I think they can rely on even more revenue streams going forward.

Potentially Entering New Waters

One thing that I haven’t covered before, and I realize now in retrospect that I should have, is the total universe that NVDA is creating with its product. At the core of NVDA, the revenues are from their GPUs like Blackwell or the upcoming Vera model. But what I’ve come to realize after writing about companies like Micron Technology (MU) working on memory chips or Celestica (CLS), which builds the server racks, is that the total addressable market for NVDA is much, much larger. With a balance sheet holding $62 billion in cash and cash equivalents, NVDA could brute force its way into these markets and sell their products as an ecosystem.

  • Blackwell GPUs

  • Grace CPUs

  • NVLink/InfiniBand Networking Switches.

Even though NVDA has a partnership with some of these companies, I don’t see why it would ignore markets that are massive. Semiconductor memory markets are, for instance, valued at over $171 billion now and are expected to grow by another 10.5% this year. Server racks are the same, valued at $107 billion last year, growing 17.7% this year. Why wouldn’t NVDA try to address these markets as well and offer a complete end-to-end product lineup, sort of mimicking the same successful ecosystem that Apple (AAPL) has created with their products.

This process has already sort of started. For instance, with server racking, NVDA is already selling the GB200 NVL72 systems, which are entire rack architectures that hold the GPUs that get plugged into the servers. By offering this as well, NVDA is slowly turning into something more like a data center blueprint vendor than a single specialized designer of GPUs.

Then for memory, which right now might still be the “hottest” industry relating to AI, with companies like SanDisk (SNDK) and MU surging in value because of the bottleneck that occurred. Supply drained, and frankly, the technology wasn’t there to meet the drastic improvements that NVDA was making on a consistent basis to their models. I still think this will continue to be a bottleneck because without faster data transfers and memory capacity, NVDA’s latest GPUs won't be able to be utilized to 100%. That might actually pose a risk for NVDA, because if companies can’t get maximum value, why pay a premium for the latest version and instead just settle for the second best and get a better “bang for your buck,” so to speak?

The Networking and Software Bull Case Worth Discussing

Once settled it can be hard to get out, partly because of something called CUDA which is at this point a 18 year old software platform which lets developers interact with the output and performance of the chips.

But what might produce the more meaningful new revenue stream for NVDA is networking and software instead. I mentioned this in my last article, and it’s yet another thing that solidifies the ecosystem of NVDA. Networking was actually the fastest-growing end market for the company over the last 12 months. It grew at 141% YoY, while the compute market grew by 58.9% instead. Still massive growth, but Networking is quickly becoming one of the areas to watch the most. This segment concerns mostly the Spectrum-X, which enables the many GPUs within a data center to more efficiently talk to each other, reducing communication bottlenecks.

Again, something that will only scale with the more chips that NVDA sells. It makes little sense to use a different network platform than what the maker of the chips is providing, I think. It’s almost what you’d see in SaaS companies when they measure the “net dollar retention.” NVDA is selling the base (the GPU) and then offering additional services like Spectrum-X, networking gear, or NVIDIA AI Enterprise software licenses.

This table is a ballpark of where the NDR would be at. In reality it would be even higher because the software pricing is only for one year. Assuming that a company would use a GPU for 4 years, reflecting the “useful lifespan” of one GPU, the Software add-on price would be closer to $18,000 per GPU sold instead. This means the NDR jumps to 177% instead ($62,000 total value/$35,000 base cost). That customers are opting for these services seems to be directly reflected in the growth of both networking and software revenues, as seen below.

We are on track to ship Spectrum-X this quarter.

That quote was from February 2024, when NVDA held their earnings call. The growth has been nothing but incredible since then. Still, I don’t think the market is fully valuing just how meaningful this is for the bottom line.

your annualized run rate on your Spectrum-X Ethernet switching platform was around $10 billion annualized. It looks like that may have stepped up to around $11 billion, $12 billion in the second half of last year.

With an annual run rate of now $12 billion, it has created meaningful new cash flows for the business. Just 2 quarters ago, in Q2 FY2026, the run rate was $10 billion. This implies a QoQ growth rate of 10% or 40% per year. It’s safe to assume, then, that by this point next year, Spectrum-X would net $16.8 billion in run-rate revenues.

We also made great progress with our software and services offerings, which reached an annualized revenue run rate of $1 billion in Q4.

The fact that software has also scaled strongly, reportedly having a $1 billion run rate in Q4 FY2024, proves that both sides of the NDR equation I laid out before are actually happening now.

In the last quarter. The non-GAAP gross margins jumped to 75.2%. Because this was during the same period that networking surged in terms of revenues, now making up 16.1% of data center revenues on an annualized basis, up from 11.2% the year before. It’s safe to assume that gross margins for Spectrum-X are above 75.2% at the moment, as it managed to lift the consolidated average. That is $13.1 billion in gross profits, assuming a 78% gross margin.

Given that this is nearly a SaaS model, the costs following here, like OPEX and other costs, are likely minimal, meaning a net margin of 65-70% is likely reasonable. That represents about $11.3 billion in net income on the midrange. NVDA reported $120 billion in net income last year, so this doesn't represent a majority of that, but it will certainly add a lot if it can continue to scale at 40% per year, reflecting the overall GPU sales of NVDA.

But the most important piece of information here, I think, is just how much Spectrum-X sales adds to the NDR. Nearly 25% of the base cost of a GPU. And for a hyperscaler, not having Spectrum if you are buying GPUs from NVDA just isn’t an option; it's essential.

The difference of -- when you build a $10 billion or $20 billion AI factory, the difference of 10%, and it could be easily 20% on the effectiveness and the through -- the utilization of your network for your data center, that translates to real money.

As CEO Jensen Huang put it in the last earnings call, paying the premium for Spectrum-X to raise data center efficiency is, at the end of the day, always worth it because it will translate into higher bottom-line numbers. Standard hardware becomes a bottleneck in the long run.

Sovereign AI - A Compelling Way To Diversify Away From Hyperscalers

In fiscal year 2026, our sovereign AI business more than tripled year-over-year and over $30 billion,

Another point worth talking about is how countries, and not just hyperscalers like Meta Platforms (META) and Amazon (AMZN), are building data centers. This creates an overreliance on a small set of companies, not just for a country but also for NVDA, as its group of customers is at the moment quite small. In FY2026, NVDA mentioned that the largest customer represents 22% of revenues and the second largest 14%.

For a company this large, that is a concerningly high concentration. This is why sovereign AI is becoming, at least for me now, one of the most important growth areas for the company. If it can diversify away from this and sign more government supply deals, it would immediately de-risk the thesis, I think. These would likely add serious revenue visibility as well, as they could span over several years.

Valuation

Just looking at the FWD valuation, the numbers might not look that appealing and are part of why I won’t rate the stock a Strong Buy yet. Non-GAAP P/E sits at 24x on an FWD basis. Assuming that NVDA can grow its EPS by 20% at least (seems highly reasonable considering the demand) between 2026 and 2027, the EPS would be $10 in 2027. I get this by using this year's EPS estimates of $8.34. So the 2027 P/E would be only 20.08x. Now it’s starting to look a lot more appealing, I think.

Companies like NVDA, which display a seriously strong MOAT and a lot of NDR potential as I’ve laid out, I think deserve a premium valuation as well. The sector median is 24x earnings right now; a 20% premium to this seems only fair. This implies a fair multiple of 28.8x. Going back to my 2027 EPS estimate of $10, the target price I have set out is about $280 per share, about 40% higher than today's levels. Spread out over 2 years means a 20% return, which I am very happy with right now. But again, even at a valuation in line with the sector, i.e., 24x earnings gives me a target price of $240. Still, this would provide about 10% annual returns, which to me is enough to justify a Buy rating.

The reason I wouldn't rate the stock a buy last year really comes down to the lack of revenue diversification I saw in the reports and the large reliance on a few customers. NVDA has since been able to tackle these issues, I think very well, with software and networking growing rapidly and now more government contracts potentially being in the pipeline.

The Full Picture

I was pessimistic the last time I covered the stock, but I've changed my position to a more bullish viewpoint instead, seeing as the company has been able to address some of the concerns I had. A Hold rating seems like it was the right choice, as the price remained the same up until a few weeks ago when it mounted a strong uptrend after falling some.

The turning point for me was how NVDA is now rapidly scaling their networking segment and software as well, which enables the gross margins to grow even higher. But the important part here is that it acts as a value addition on top of the core business, which still is selling GPUs. This diversification, along with a healthy balance sheet filled with plenty of cash and an attractive valuation, only means one thing: NVDA is now a Buy.

data.title: Once Upon A Farm: A Soft Start For Investors In This Better-For-You Brand
data.description:
When Once Upon A Farm (OFRM) went public in early February, I believed that while the products were better for kids, I wondered if that was the case for investors as well.

The better-for-you food play for babies and small children posted modest losses while topline growth slowed quite a bit. Cautious of this setup and mindful of painful rides in better-for-you brands before, I decided to avoid the shares for now.

This came as, on top of the better-for-you brand, the company has been backed by a celebrity in the person of Jennifer Garner. Previous episodes in this field have been far from a success with or without the help of celebrities; for that, just ask investors in The Honest Company (HNST), for instance.

Having gone public at $18 per share and trading at $21 on the first day of trading, Once Upon A Farm shares have fallen to the $15 mark here, despite quite some news flow taking place ever since.

Other interesting corporate events, including recent IPO stories, are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Fourth Quarter & Other Developments

In the middle of March, Once Upon A Farm reported a 30% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $64.0 million, that number coming in a million ahead of the preliminary guidance.

While the growth place looks solid, it compares to a more than 53% increase in full-year sales to more than $240 million, and while volumes were up 42%, that implies a big contribution from pricing as well. Most of the fourth quarter growth is driven by a near 62% increase in baby sales to more than $29 million, with kids sales up 11% to nearly $35 million.

While full-year operating losses of $5.7 million were pretty flat compared to 2024, fourth-quarter operating profits of $4.5 million looked relatively strong.

At the time of the offering, I pegged the pro forma share count at 40 million, yet the press release indicated that this came in close to 42 million due to the IPO overallotment option. This makes for a $630 million equity valuation here. The company ended 2025 with a net debt load of $50 million, or $82 million if derivatives are included. This, however, is ahead of the IPO proceeds, which were reported at $139 million, for roughly a $60 million pro forma net cash position, or a $570 million operating asset valuation.

This values the business at 2.4 times mostly break-even sales, as the fourth quarter has been a bit stronger, not just from a sales perspective but also from a margin perspective, which is comforting. In that light, the guidance is of crucial importance.

The company issued a comforting $302-$310 million revenue guidance for 2026, which implies 25%-29% annual growth, and EBITDA is seen between positive $2 and $4 million. That guidance is precisely the problem, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $2 million for all of 2025, which suggests no real operational leverage seen this year, despite a continuation of solid sales growth. This comes after adjusted EBITDA improved by nearly $6 million in 2025.

Following the earnings report, the company launched five new products featuring protein in March, and in April the company achieved WIC approval in the state of California, both encouraging news events.

Some Perspective

The idea of the business is to drive fundamental and systemic change in childhood nutrition by providing real, organic, and farm-fresh food that does not contain added sugars, preservatives, or other artificial supplements. These foods are seen in the forms of pouches, frozen meals, oat bars, and baby snacks, sold through coolers.

Risks outside the general business risks include the availability of organic, plant-based, and natural ingredients; customer concentration; weather; and food contamination risks, among others. The biggest issue in the outlook is a lack of operating leverage, in my view, driven by a deliberate strategy of investment in the organization and infrastructure. Part of this infrastructure is an increase in the number of coolers around, but total capital expenditures only come in at $5 million in 2025, marking this not a key determinant of the P&L in my view.

Hence the deleveraging in terms of EBITDA and its margins is what holds back the shares in my view, although the company of course will incur costs from being a publicly listed business going forward. Hence, this is that situation that I feared at the time of the offering. Following shares pulling back a third and the 2026 sales guidance being solid, this results in sales multiples looking more compelling. Amidst no realistic margins, there are no few immediate triggers to get upbeat yet, in my view.

data.title: Selloff May Be Brewing; Hedge Before Big Tech's Earnings
data.description:
Since late October of last year, the SPY has basically gone nowhere. It was sitting in this tight range for months, grinding sideways. Then the Iran conflict started, and the market started to tank. SPY dropped about 9% from its ATHs at the time. And Cross-asset volatility went haywire across stocks, FX, and commodities. It felt like the beginning of something much worse.

But then, as these things usually go, headlines about ceasefire talks and de-escalation started hitting the news. And at the end of March, we began to recover, and when I say recover, I mean we went basically vertical.

Since April 1st, the SPY is up more than 12%. The Nasdaq (NDX) is up more than 18%. In less than a month. These indexes are stretched like a rubber band. Now, there are a bunch of articles here on Seeking Alpha right now making the "Sell in May and Go Away" arguments. I have read several of them, and I respect the reasoning.

One piece I read laid out some good arguments about midterm seasonality and the new Fed chair risk that I think are genuinely worth considering. But I respectfully disagree with the conclusion of actually selling your core index positions.

My investing philosophy when it comes to participating in the S&P 500 through ETFs like the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO), and Nasdaq Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ), is that you buy and never sell, ever. If you are holding an index fund and you are up on it with unrealized gains, selling that to time a pullback is, in my opinion, one of the worst things a retail investor can do.

Because you have to be right twice. You have to time the exit and the re-entry too. So instead of telling you to sell, I want to share with you something I think is far more rational. A portfolio hedging strategy that lets you keep your positions intact while reducing your downside in the event of a major selloff if that happens over the next 6 months.

Because the macro setup right now is telling us that there is a risk of mean reversion. And it is much higher than the market is pricing in. I think it is a very good time to hedge because it is cheaper to hedge when everyone is in a FOMO state.

Set Up For Mean Reversion

I think that this rally is overextended, and the next few months carry more downside risk than the current sentiments. A gain of 12% in the S&P 500 (SPX) in under a month is not normal. Markets do not move in straight lines. The selloff in March was overdone on fear, and the rally in this month is overdone on FOMO.

When the market bottomed at the end of March, the option market was initially skeptical of the rally. Most of the activity was people fading it by selling calls and resetting hedges. But since April 8th, that has flipped dramatically.

SPX one-month call skew (demand for upside calls) is up from near a one-year low to the 90th percentile high. But the Put skew has fallen to the 15th percentile low over the past year, meaning hedgers are capitulating. Which is highly unusual to me. We usually see more puts trading than calls.

Overall SPX option volumes hit a record of 6.7 million contracts on Friday alone, roughly $4.7 trillion in notional value. Due to the high demand of calls, this creates a perfect reversion environment for the dealers/market makers to fade the market to hedge their position against the calls they sold. And keep in mind that there is an upcoming triple witching event on June 19th where all the major options expire on a single day, which means the hedging pressure in upcoming months will be higher than normal.

But there is a divergence I want to highlight. While the VIX Index has fallen from its March highs, single stock volatility as measured by the VIXEQSM Index is still just 5 points off its March peak. What this tells me is that while the market looks calm, individual stock risk is still high. And that matters a lot going into this earnings cycle because these mega-cap names move the entire index with them.

On top of that, there is a Fed chair transition coming. Kevin Warsh’s nomination is working its way through the Senate. There is a well-documented history of the stock market "testing" new Fed chairs. It happened with Jerome Powell in October 2018 when hawkish comments triggered a selloff. If the market decides to test Warsh, that adds another layer of volatility on top of everything else.

Big Tech Earnings

The next week is going to set the tone for the market for at least the next six months, possibly longer. Four of the five Big Tech names report earnings starting April 29th — Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Amazon.co, Inc. (AMZN), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT). The question the market is going to ask and is already starting to ask is whether the returns on these investments are materializing fast enough.

And the honest answer is that we do not know yet. If even one of these companies signals in the upcoming earnings calls that they are reassessing the pace of spending or that AI monetization is progressing slower than expected, then the repricing will be violent.

Alphabet

The market expects revenue of $106.9 billion with 18% YoY growth. In Q4 it delivered $113 billion in revenue with 18% growth, so even with accounting for seasonality, delivering something close to $110 billion seems very achievable. What I am focused on is the CapEx number. They guided 2026 CapEx to $185 billion, which frightened people enough to get EPS estimates lowered to $2.62.

Last quarter, cash from operations was about $164 billion against $91 billion in CapEx. I am expecting a scenario where Q1 Capex alone eats 80% to 100% of cash from operations. The market could react negatively even if the top line beats. Also, if their Google Cloud business sees a margin compression because of the Capex ramp, even strong revenue growth might not be enough to save the stock in the near term.

Amazon

Amazon is the one I am most worried about from a valuation standpoint. After the Anthropic deal news, Amazon ripped about 31% from March lows to around $264. At the current market cap of $2.84 trillion, I think the stock is stretched. For Q1, they guided to 11 to 15% top line growth and net sales expected between $173.5 billion and $178.5 billion. By segment, the growth rate may be largely by AWS, which posted 24% constant currency growth in the last quarter.

I believe that the AWS growth is solid, and the Anthropic deal plus OpenAI's $100 billion AWS commitment are solid tailwinds. But in my opinion, this growth is already priced in. If AWS growth comes in below 25% or if management gives any signal about the capacity limiting growth, the stock does not have valuation room to absorb that.

Meta Platforms

Meta is the Big Tech name I think has the best chance of delivering a clean earnings beat. They have not missed estimates since 2022. And not small beats either. EPS surprises have been 7% or greater in 11 of those quarters, and revenue surprises have averaged over $1 billion in the last five quarters.

For Q1, Wall Street expects 31.3% YoY revenue growth, which would be the first 30%+ quarterly growth since Q3 of 2021. They previously guided Q1 revenue between $53.5 billion and $56.5 billion. I expect the EPS growth to be small at about 5% YoY due to the drag from AI investments.

What makes me somewhat positive on Meta going into the print is that their AI investments are already being commercialized and generating measurable revenue gains. In Q4, model improvements drove increases in clicks, conversions, and ad quality without increasing ad load. If that trend continues in Q1, it supports the argument that Meta's growth is coming from better monetization, not just more ads.

The risk here is the Capex guidance. Like every other hyperscaler, they are ramping spending aggressively through 2026. If the market decides to punish Capex across the board, Meta will not be spared, even with a beat.

Microsoft

Microsoft is the most interesting setup of the four because the stock has been beaten down hard since late 2025, and expectations are already low. The consensus for Q3 is $81.4 billion in revenue (+16.18% YoY) and $4.07 in adjusted EPS (+17.7% YoY).

The key number I will be watching is Azure growth. They guided Q3 Azure growth to 37% to 38%, which at the low end would be a sequential deceleration from Q2. The capacity limit is the only thing limiting Azure's upside, and they have been addressing this by signing over $60 billion in deals with neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius to scale capacity faster.

If we see Azure growth above 38%, I would see this as a big positive. What I find compelling about Microsoft at current levels is the valuation. MSFT still trades at less than 25x forward earnings, well below its 10 year average of 33x.

They also guided for OpEx to grow at only 10 to 11% in Q3, much below revenue growth. If the operating leverage shows up, EPS could beat by more than the market expects. But Capex is still ramping, and depreciation from prior quarters is rolling through COGS, so gross margins could face pressure. If Microsoft reports a clean beat with Azure acceleration, this stock could rerate quickly to the upside.

How To Hedge Your Portfolio

Now this is the part I actually wanted to write this whole article for. Because I see a lot of people talking about hedging, and the advice usually comes down to "buy some puts." I don’t see that as a hedge. It is a directional bet with a time decay problem. A real hedging strategy needs something that works to protect you across different scenarios.

But before I get into the hedging strategy, I have built an example portfolio based on what I think a tech-tilted but not recklessly concentrated portfolio looks like. This is not my actual portfolio, but it is an example to show you guys how the hedging strategy works in practice.

This portfolio has about 62% direct tech and AI exposure when you include the components of QQQ and SPY plus the stock positions. The remaining exposure is spread across healthcare and financials like JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM). The 13% cash gives us dry powder to buy dips if they happen, but it does not protect the 87% that is deployed. That is what the hedging strategy is for.

So, for this portfolio, the total budget for the hedge is $14 thousand, which is 2.8% of the total portfolio value. I am structuring this hedge with 2 legs, each for a different purpose. The time horizon is five months, meaning we are positioning for a potential selloff in the next five months.

Delta Hedge, ATM SPY Puts (Cost: $10,000)

This would be our core protection. My goal here is to directly offset losses in my largest positions if the market pulls back 5 to 15% from here.

I am buying 4 contracts of SPY put options at the $705 strike with about 165 days to expiration. At current implied volatility levels and SPY trading around $710, these contracts should cost roughly $25 per share. Which is about $10,100 of total debit.

These 4 contracts would give us a notional protection on about $282k of SPY exposure. Our actual SPY position is $42,500, but the QQQ position and tech holdings are highly correlated to SPY. So basically these puts are giving us a delta hedge on a much larger portion of our portfolio than just the SPY part.

The reason I am choosing near the money puts is because they have a high absolute delta. They move close to the underlying (Pnl wise) when it drops through the strike. OTM puts are cheaper, but they also do nothing for you in a 5 to 8% pullback. This leg is our active protection for the most probable negative scenario, which is a small correction.

VIX Call Spread As A Vol Hedge (Cost: $3,500)

This is the leg that many people never think about, and it is the most important one in a crisis. When the market sells off, implied volatility spikes. The VIX can go from 17 to 35 in a week. And when that happens, VIX call options can multiply in value 5x or even 10x depending on the strike and the severity of the move. VIX calls are the most powerful way to hedge a sudden vol shock.

But you do not want to just buy naked VIX calls because VIX options have some unique problems. The VIX term structure is usually in contango, meaning longer dated VIX futures trade above spot. This creates a constant problem for long VIX positions because of roll decay. To manage this, I am using a call spread.

  • Buy 12 contracts of VIX $20 calls with a September 2026 expiry.

  • Sell 12 contracts of VIX $38 calls Same expiry.

The $20/$38 call spread on the VIX with September expiry should cost roughly $3.5k on these 12 contracts. Your maximum gain on this trade is capped at $18.4 thousand if the VIX goes above 38, which is exactly the kind of move you would see in a market panic.

Even if the VIX only gets to 30, the spread would be worth about $8 thousand, like in March when the Iran conflict escalated and the VIX went over 30. In a scenario where Big Tech earnings disappoint, a move to 35 to 40 on the VIX is very probable. This leg is our "force multiplier" that will pay off precisely when our positions are getting hurt the most.

The reason I cap it at $38 with the short call is to reduce the cost. Selling the $38 call funds a portion of the $20 call purchase, making the spread cheaper. But you give up the unlimited upside above VIX 40. Because if the VIX is above 40, your other leg is already doing plenty of work for you.

But if you want more exposure to an unexpected vol shock, you can make this call spread unbalanced by buying 17 call contracts of VIX at a $20 strike and shorting just 12 contracts at $38. This will, of course, cost more, but now this will offset your drawdown and PnL much faster in the event of a sudden big selloff.

What Would PnL Look Like After Hedging

Let's say Big Tech earnings disappoint or some combination of macro events hits, and the market drops 10% from here over the next two to three months. Your $435k in deployed equity is now down about $43.5 thousand.

But here is what your hedge would be doing. The 4 ATM SPY puts at the $705 strike are now deep in the money with SPY trading around $640. At this point those puts would have gained about $16k in value. That alone would be recovering more than a third of your drawdown.

In a 10% market selloff over a short timeframe, the VIX is going to spike. It is almost mechanical. In a 10% drawdown, a VIX at 35 is very reasonable. At 35, your call spread would have gained roughly $9 thousand in value. So, your combined hedge PnL would be about $25 thousand against a $43.5 thousand drawdown. This way you have offset more than half of the damage. Your net drawdown would be about 4.3% instead of 10%.

Now, if you make the spread unbalanced, meaning you buy 17 contracts of the VIX $20 calls but only sell 12 contracts at the $38 strike. This costs more upfront, maybe another $2 thousand, but in the same scenario with VIX at 35, those five extra contracts would be adding another $7.5 thousand in gains.

Your total PnL from this hedge would be about $32.5 thousand against the $43.5 thousand drawdown. Your effective drawdown goes from 10% to about 2.5% for a cost of roughly $15.5 thousand in total hedge cost.

If Market Keeps Running Upwards

Let's say Big Tech crushes earnings and the market takes all of this and runs with it. SPY moves another 5% higher, maybe more. In this case, your ATM SPY puts are decaying every single day. They are losing value both from directional movement and from theta decay. My advice here is do not just sit there and watch $10k bleed to zero.

If within the first 35 to 40 days after you put the hedge on, the market confirms a bullish regime and you are okay with the risk, close the ATM puts. Yes, you will take a loss on them. But getting back $5 thousand of your $10 thousand is much better than letting the whole thing expire worthless. But I would not close the VIX spread.

The reason is that VIX tends to spike suddenly and without warning. Let's say the market moves 10% higher over the next three months, and everything looks fine. Then some unexpected event hits, and the VIX spikes to 30 in a single week. This call spread that you paid $3,500 for would be sitting at $7 to $8 thousand in value. You can close it then on some good gains or at least break even on the entire hedge after the loss you took on the puts.

Final Thoughts

If you are averaging dollar cost into index funds and you are up on your positions, that is compounding working in your favor, and my advice is that you should not interrupt compounding. But what you can do, and what I think you should seriously consider doing right now, is protect the compounding.

The next week, Big Tech earnings will set the narrative for the second half of 2026. If they crush it, then you paid 2.8% for insurance you did not need. If they don't, or if the market decides to ask some tough questions about the CapEx, then this hedge could be the difference between a painful drawdown and a manageable one.

data.title: GBUG: Gold Miners Still Look Undervalued And Have Significant Upside Potential
data.description:
The Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF (GBUG) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund that aims to provide its investors with long-term capital appreciation by investing in the common equities of gold-focused and silver-focused mining companies. As this ETF focuses on capital appreciation, it may not be as attractive to income investors as a fund such as the GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (GGN) or the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust (GNT), the latter of which we discussed in an article published on Seeking Alpha a few days ago. However, the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF still manages to boast a 1.45% yield at the current price, which compares pretty well with the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and most other American broad-market indices. Furthermore, this fund does not require investors to sacrifice some of the capital appreciation that its underlying assets deliver, as the income-focused gold mining funds do. As such, this fund could very well deliver stronger total returns than the closed-end funds in a bull market. As many gold and silver miners appear to be undervalued right now, this could make GBUG the better investment.

About The Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF

The website for the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF offers the following description of the fund:

Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF is an actively managed ETF that aims to provide long-term capital appreciation by investing in shares of gold- and silver-focused companies that are engaged in exploring, developing and mining, or royalty and streaming companies engaged in the financing of gold and silver assets. The investment strategy of the Fund is value-oriented and contrarian.

The fact that the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF is an actively managed fund makes it something of a rarity in the exchange-traded fund space. Passively managed exchange-traded funds have been around since the 1990s, but actively managed ETFs have only been available in the United States since 2008. Actively managed funds are still much rarer than exchange-traded index funds, which might be due to many investors being fairly cost-conscious. As actively managed ETFs actually have a human management team that needs to be compensated for their work, these funds tend to be more expensive than passively managed index funds that do not need to have human management on staff. With that said, though, the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF only has an expense ratio of 0.89%, which is not really too bad. It is, however, higher than the 0.51% gross expense ratio of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX).

While some investors dislike high expense ratios, I personally do not mind them, as I am only really concerned about the fund’s performance after these expenses are paid. After all, this is the performance that investors in a given fund will end up receiving. In this respect, the Sprott Active Gold and Silver Miners ETF does not disappoint. The fund has an inception date of February 19, 2025. Since that date, GBUG has delivered a 134.79% total return. This beats both the VanEck Gold Miners ETF and the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR):

In fact, we can see that the S&P 500 Total Return Index almost appears as though it stood still next to gold miners over the past fourteen months or so. This is partly because gold (XAUUSD:CUR) itself was one of the best-performing asset classes in 2025:

There are some sources on the Internet that claim that gold was the best-performing asset in 2025. This is actually not correct, as both silver (XAGUSD:CUR) and platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) outperformed gold by quite a lot in 2025:

However, all three precious metals did substantially outperform common stocks. This had a beneficial impact on the share prices of precious metals mining companies over the same period. Newmont (NEM) is the only gold mining company in the S&P 500 Index. Over the course of 2025, Newmont’s stock increased in price by 168.27% (far more than the index):

This was sufficient for it to be one of the ten best-performing stocks in the index. Its performance greatly exceeded the 21.21% gain of the “Magnificent 7” technology stocks (MAGS) as a group, despite the fact that the “Magnificent 7” technology stocks were far more commonly mentioned in the financial media. Many analysts who actively follow the gold market, including myself, suspect that the performance of gold as an asset class (and, by extension, gold mining stocks) is ignored by much of the financial media because gold is arguably something of a vote of no confidence in the fiat currency system. I will admit that I have been actively purchasing physical gold because I have some concerns about the financial system and the current outlook for many of the world’s developed economies, which will be discussed later in this article. However, I see gold miners (such as the ones included in the GBUG ETF) as an investment in companies that produce a product just like any other. Their revenue and cash flows typically rise whenever gold prices do, so in that respect, these companies have a link to gold, but ultimately, all that they are doing is extracting gold from the ground and selling it in exchange for fiat currency. An increase in the price of gold tends to have a beneficial effect on the results of gold miners, which we saw in Newmont’s first-quarter 2026 earnings results.

This chart shows the largest positions in the portfolio of the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF:

We can see that the fund has a position in Newmont, but it is not the fund’s largest position. This is in spite of the fact that Newmont is the largest gold mining company in the world. This immediately tells us that the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF is not making any real effort to ensure that its portfolio is market-capitalization weighted, and it is certainly not one of those funds that claims to be actively managed while actually holding a portfolio that resembles an index. As we are paying for active management here, the fact that this fund does not simply invest in a portfolio that is similar to an index is nice to see.

Indeed, we can see that the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF is very different from that of a gold mining index fund by comparing GBUG’s portfolio to that of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF, which is an index fund. Here are the largest positions in GDX’s portfolio:

While there are a few companies that are included in the portfolios of both funds, we can see that the weightings of nearly every company are different between the two funds. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF had 30% of its assets invested in just three companies – Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Newmont, and Barrick Mining (B) – as of April 23, 2026. Meanwhile, the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF only has a single position that accounts for more than 5% of its portfolio, and that position – G Mining Ventures (GMINF) – does not account for much more than 5% of the fund’s assets. Thus, the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF appears to be somewhat more diversified. This could be a real advantage, given that some governments around the world have, in the past, expropriated resources owned by foreign companies. If this were to happen to a company that accounts for a sizable proportion of a fund’s assets, it would likely result in large losses for investors. In addition, there is always a risk that a mining company will spend a great deal of money exploring for or even constructing a gold mine only to find out that there is less gold available at the site than was expected. This can have an adverse effect on the stock price of a mining company. If the fund has outsized exposure to a company that this happens to, investors in the fund could suffer large losses. This is less of a risk if the fund does not have much exposure to the asset in question. As such, a well-diversified fund such as GBUG could be less risky to investors than GDX. Obviously, anyone who is concerned about risk will likely find this particular characteristic to be attractive.

As we have already seen, gold miners in general delivered very strong performance over the past year. This was largely driven by the rapid increase in gold prices that we have seen over the past three years or so. Despite this, many gold miners are still very reasonably priced. We can see this by looking at the forward price-to-earnings ratios of the largest holdings in GBUG’s portfolio. Here they are:

As gold prices have increased rapidly over the past year, it is likely that the forward price-to-earnings figures are much lower than the figures provided.

As of the time of writing, the S&P 500 Index has a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 27.24 and a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 22.44. We can immediately see that most of the companies listed have much more attractive valuations, in spite of the fact that they have delivered very strong growth and very strong stock price performance over the past year. If the price of gold continues to rise over the remainder of this year, then it is very possible that investors in these companies will end up paying even less per dollar of earnings that they produce than the forward price-to-earnings figures suggest. As of right now, most investment banks are predicting that gold prices will be much higher at the end of the year than the current $4,709.27 per ounce. We can see that in this table:

Bank

HSBC is the only bank that thinks that gold could decline from today’s level. The catalyst for the downside that the bank’s analyst provided is a significant easing in geopolitical tensions and a substantial reduction in U.S. and other developed nation government spending. While he is correct that these two events could cause gold prices to decline, I have a very hard time believing that either one of these scenarios will actually occur. The Ukraine-Russia War has prompted European governments to increase military spending, and U.S. President Trump’s proposed fiscal year 2027 budget asks for a 42% increase in U.S. military spending. This points to higher developed nation fiscal spending and heightened geopolitical tensions, not less. This points to higher gold prices by the end of the year, not at all consistent with HSBC’s scenario.

One metric that can be used to predict the profitability of mining companies is to look at All-In Sustaining Costs. Investing.com offers the following definition of this metric:

All-in Sustaining Cost represents the total cost of an ounce of gold, including all direct mining expenses, administrative overhead, and capital expenditures required to sustain current production levels.

Thus, All-In Sustaining Cost is the amount of money that it costs a mining company to produce a single ounce of gold. As the definition suggests, this figure includes the direct costs of operating a mine (including equipment costs and worker salaries and benefits), indirect costs (such as corporate expenses and executive salaries), and even capital expenditures. According to Farmonaut, a data analytics company that serves the mining industry, the All-in Sustaining Costs for most gold miners range between $900 and $1,400 per ounce (Newmont’s was $1,709 per ounce in the first quarter of 2026). With gold bouncing between $4,500 and $5,000 per ounce, and potentially going higher, it becomes very easy to see how the companies held in the portfolio of the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF could be very profitable going forward.

As we saw with Newmont’s first-quarter earnings a few days ago, there is a very real possibility that Wall Street analysts are using revenue projections that are far too low for most mining companies. Last year, Morningstar stated that its valuation models assume a gold price of $3,170 per ounce for the 2025 to 2027 period and $2,000 per ounce through 2029. That is far lower than what any mining company is receiving for its production right now. Any valuation model using that figure will produce revenue and earnings figures that are well below what the companies will actually end up receiving and earning. Newmont’s first-quarter 2026 financial results showed an 11.26% revenue beat and a 27.04% non-GAAP earnings per share beat, which suggests that Wall Street analysts are still assuming that the realized price of gold that miners receive for their production is well below today’s actual price of gold. All the forward price-to-earnings ratios shown in the table above are calculated based on Wall Street analysts’ models. Thus, many gold mining companies are actually cheaper than the already very cheap forward price-to-earnings ratios suggest, due to the simple fact that the earnings per share that are being used to calculate the ratios are lower than what the companies will likely end up reporting. This points to higher share prices for the companies in GBUG’s portfolio as further earnings beats are announced going forward.

The Case For Higher Gold Prices

As was already mentioned, the revenues and profits of gold mining companies tend to increase whenever the price of gold rises. In most cases, this will have a positive impact on the stock prices of those companies. The Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF invests in the common equities of gold and silver miners and uses an authorized participant to keep its own share price in line with the price of the assets underlying each share of the fund. As such, we can expect increases in the price of gold to be a driver of higher prices for this fund’s shares.

With that said, the share prices of gold miners do not exhibit a perfect correlation with the price of gold. This chart shows the spot price of gold alongside the price of the MarketVector Global Gold Miners Index over the past ten years:

There is certainly a very high correlation between the price of gold and the gold miners index. However, there are periods of time in which the equity prices of gold miners lagged behind that of gold itself. However, there is a strong enough correlation between the two to conclude that investors in gold miners should make money whenever the price of gold rises. As such, we should take a look at some of the fundamentals of gold as we seek to formulate a thesis for the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF. Fortunately for us, the case for higher gold prices going forward is quite strong.

Historically, gold has acted as a monetary asset and a store of wealth. As the Federal Reserve itself points out:

In 1913 the gold standard was built into the framework of the Federal Reserve. The law required the Federal Reserve to hold gold equal to 40 percent of the value of the currency it issued and to convert those dollars into gold at a fixed price of $20.67 per ounce of pure gold.

As the Federal Reserve admits, the law that created it required the American central bank to have enough gold at all times to fully convert 40% of the currency into gold at a price of $20.67. This law was amended in 1933 to reduce the amount of gold that the Federal Reserve had to keep on hand to back the currency, but it still required the nation’s central bank to hold gold. In other words, the laws surrounding the creation of the Federal Reserve explicitly established gold as a monetary asset. Many other nations around the world either explicitly require their central banks to hold gold or allow them to do so, which further supports the idea that gold is a monetary asset.

As of today, the Federal Reserve does not have anywhere near enough gold to back 40% of the issued currency with gold. Actual opinions on the size of the U.S. money supply vary depending on what is being measured. The Federal Reserve tracks a few different measures:

  • M0: The total value of all physical cash in circulation plus the balances held in the central bank’s reserve accounts.
  • M1: The total value of M0 plus demand deposits at banks. This is theoretically the amount of money that could be spent immediately.
  • M2: The total value of M1 plus cash-equivalent securities held in retail money funds and small time deposits. This money could still be spent very quickly if its owners wished to do so, but it is not quite as liquid as M1.
  • M3: The total value of M2 plus large time deposits. The Federal Reserve does not publish the M3 figure anymore, but arguably, it should because all of the components of M3 are still technically in the system and invested in something. A bank does not just let them sit in the vault and pay interest on large time deposits; it lends it out to other individuals and businesses.

The most comprehensive measure of the money supply is M3, but the most comprehensive measure that is actually being published in the United States is M2. As of the end of February 2026 (the most recent date for which data is available), the U.S. dollar-denominated M2 money supply is $22.5722 trillion. If that was backed 40% by gold, as the original law creating the Federal Reserve requires, then the Federal Reserve would need to have $9.02888 trillion worth of gold. The Gold Reserve Act of 1934 required the Federal Reserve to transfer all of its gold to the U.S. Treasury Department. However, the Federal Reserve does provide custody service for some of the U.S. Treasury Department’s gold, even today. According to the U.S. Treasury Department, the Federal Reserve had 13,452,433.111 troy ounces of gold in custody that is owned by the U.S. Treasury Department:

At today’s price of gold, that is valued at $63.361 billion. Clearly, that is nowhere near enough to provide 40% backing of the M2 money supply. However, the amount of gold that the U.S. Treasury has on deposit with the Federal Reserve is only a fraction of the gold owned by the U.S. Treasury Department. The majority of the gold is held at the U.S. Mints in Denver, Colorado; Fort Knox, Kentucky; and West Point, New York. The combined total amount of gold owned by the U.S. Treasury Department between all of these locations is 452,694,491.902 troy ounces. At today’s gold price, that is worth approximately $2.132 trillion. That is still not enough to provide 40% gold backing for the currency as envisioned by the drafters of the 1913 law. In order to provide the same money supply backing as the original Federal Reserve Act required, the price of gold would need to be $19,944.75 per ounce. At its current price of $4,710 per ounce, gold is significantly undervalued relative to the U.S. dollar based on the historical relationship between the two.

At this point, some readers might argue that the United States is not on the gold standard anymore. For that matter, no country in the world is currently using the gold standard. As such, that comparison may not be relevant. While that is certainly a valid argument, I would argue that gold has been a monetary asset for nearly 6,000 years. We should not completely ignore the history, as gold has a place in many cultures, and the long history of gold as a store of value means that people may turn to it as a vehicle for wealth preservation if trust between nations or individuals begins to break down. If this were to occur, the price would rise, and the profits of gold mining companies would rise right along with it.

We should not ignore the fact that M2 has been rising more rapidly than gross domestic product in recent years. Indeed, this has been the case for most of the 21st century. For December of 2000, the Federal Reserve reports that the M2 money supply was $4.95555 trillion. Today, that figure is $22.5722 trillion:

That is a compound annual growth rate of roughly 6.2%. U.S. gross domestic product growth has not been anywhere close to that number over the same period. The fourth-quarter 2000 gross domestic product estimate for the United States was $10.435744 trillion, whereas the most recent figure today is $31.422526 trillion:

That is only a 4.51% compound annual growth rate over the same period. Thus, the M2 money supply grew more rapidly than the actual productive output of the economy. Not only is this inflationary, but it suggests that a substantial proportion of the economic growth in the United States over the past 25 years was driven by monetary expansion. This could actually explain why gold has outperformed the U.S. stock market over the same period. Over the period from December 29, 2000, through April 24, 2026, gold is up 1,688.58%, but the S&P 500 Index has only gained 448.86%:

Gold as an asset class produces nothing. It is literally just a shiny yellow metal. In an economy driven by organic growth rather than monetary expansion, common stocks, which represent ownership of actual productive companies, should outperform a mass of metal sitting on a shelf or in a vault. That was not the case (and it was not the case even if we assume that a stock investor reinvested all of the dividends paid out by every S&P 500 constituent company over the same period).

The other thing that we can derive from this analysis is that it now takes more than $1 of new money creation in order to produce $1 of gross domestic product growth. In the current monetary system, all new money must be borrowed into existence. This means that debt must grow faster than gross domestic product. This points to a continued rise in the price of gold going forward. The reason for this is very simple: gold is the only monetary asset with no counterparty risk. A piece of physical bullion that an investor has in their possession cannot default. This is not true of any other asset. Admittedly, at this point, some readers might point out that a country that has its own currency and its own central bank does not need to default on its sovereign bonds unless it wants to. That is true, but there is no guarantee that the fiat currency that it pays out to its bondholders will have the same purchasing power as the fiat currency that was originally lent. The face value of a bond declines in real terms over time unless there is no inflation over the term of a bond, which does not seem like a realistic scenario whenever most central banks around the world aim to have at least some inflation over time. As we have just seen, gold should do a much better job of holding its value in real terms than any bond (although there is no guarantee that its price will always remain stable in real terms during a given period of time). With this in mind, it should be no surprise that central banks around the world have been increasing their gold holdings in recent years, to the point where gold now exceeds U.S. Treasury holdings on the balance sheets of central banks in aggregate. These central banks are simply trying to protect their national wealth in real terms.

It is almost certain that the money supply in the United States will continue to grow over the coming years. During the month of February 2026, the money supply increased by 5.56% year-over-year. This was higher than the 4.90% year-over-year money supply growth in February. Furthermore, the rate of change in the money supply (measured on a year-over-year basis) has been increasing over the past three months:

This is partly due to the federal funds target rate cuts in the final four months of 2025. Another contributing factor is that the Federal Reserve returned to balance sheet expansion in December. This is very similar to the quantitative easing that took place in the years following the 2008 financial crisis, although the Federal Reserve is not calling it quantitative easing. The end result is the same, however, as the Federal Reserve is basically creating money out of thin air in order to buy $40 billion worth of U.S. Treasury securities on a monthly basis. As we can see here, the total assets on the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet have been increasing since December:

Furthermore, this increases the money supply by the value of the U.S. Treasuries that the Federal Reserve purchases. The central bank has not provided a termination date for these purchases, and the official announcement left open the possibility that the Federal Reserve will continue these purchases indefinitely. From the Federal Reserve:

The Desk anticipates that the pace of RMPs will remain elevated for a few months to offset expected large increases in non-reserve liabilities in April. After that, the pace of total purchases will likely be significantly reduced in line with expected seasonal patterns in Federal Reserve liabilities. Purchase amounts will be adjusted as appropriate based on the outlook for reserve supply and market conditions.

While the central bank does suggest that it will reduce the size of its purchases beginning in May 2026, it does not say that it ever intends to stop. Indeed, the way this statement is phrased strongly suggests that the Federal Reserve will continue to create money and purchase U.S. Treasury securities indefinitely. Furthermore, the following paragraph in the Federal Reserve’s statement gives the impression that the money supply will continue to increase going forward as a result of this program:

The Desk was also directed in October to reinvest all principal payments from the Federal Reserve’s holdings of agency securities into Treasury bills via secondary market purchases. The monthly schedule of planned purchases will include RMPs as well as these purchases.

In other words, the Federal Reserve is now using all of the proceeds that it receives whenever one of the U.S. Treasury securities that it holds matures to purchase new U.S. Treasuries. The central bank is no longer allowing the U.S. Treasury bills that it holds to mature and then removing the money from the system. It is instead using the principal payments to buy new U.S. Treasuries and creating new money to buy additional U.S. Treasuries on top of that. This points to a growing money supply going forward, providing a strong tailwind for gold.

Rising gold prices result in higher revenues and profits for gold miners, including the gold miners that are held in the portfolio of the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF. As we have already seen, there could be a good case made for the companies in the portfolio being undervalued even with gold at today’s levels. When we combine this with the potential for forward growth simply from rising gold prices, a good case can be made for owning the fund.

Distributions

As is the case with all exchange-traded funds, the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF simply collects all of the dividends that it receives from the companies in its portfolio and then pays them out to its shareholders after deducting the fund’s own expenses. This fund has an expense ratio of 0.89%, so we do, unfortunately, lose a bit of the income that we would have earned had we owned the companies in the fund’s portfolio directly. This fund’s yield is only 1.45%, but this is still better than both the S&P 500 and the VanEck Gold Miners ETF:

Fund

TTM Yield

Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF

1.45%

VanEck Gold Miners ETF

0.67%

State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY)

1.03%

Furthermore, this fund has outperformed both of the above index funds since its inception. It is thus difficult to complain about the yield, as the fund has rewarded its investors overall. Furthermore, this fund is not designed specifically for investors who want a high level of income. All that it is doing is simply holding the common stocks of gold and silver mining companies and passing the dividends that these companies pay to its own investors. Thus, the fund’s distributions will vary depending on the dividends paid by the portfolio companies. Furthermore, this is an actively managed fund with a 20% turnover rate, so its distributions will vary depending on exactly what companies it has in its portfolio during any given period. The fund makes no effort to target a specific yield, nor does it make any effort to use strategies meant to produce income for its shareholders.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF is an actively managed exchange-traded fund that invests in companies that mine gold and silver. The fund is relatively young, but it has managed to achieve a pretty impressive track record since its creation. Gold and silver miners in general have delivered strong performance over the past eighteen months, and the sector as a whole has outperformed the S&P 500 Index by a lot. Despite this, many of the companies that are held in GBUG’s portfolio still appear to be substantially undervalued. Gold prices are likely to continue to rise going forward, and the companies held in the fund’s portfolio (and the fund’s own share price) should do quite well going forward.
