data.title: American Century Focused Dynamic Growth Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

Equity markets declined during the first quarter. The war in Iran weighed heavily on markets. The Strait of Hormuz closure caused crude oil prices to soar, which ignited fears of slower economic growth, rising unemployment and high prices and lowered expectations for a Federal Reserve interest rate cut. Before the war, U.S. stocks had faced headwinds due to concerns that artificial intelligence (AI) could disrupt traditional business models, potentially leading to layoffs. Tariff policy uncertainty also added to volatility during the quarter.

Growth stocks sharply underperformed value. The recent market rotation continued before and during the war, as value stocks posted gains across the market-capitalization spectrum while growth stocks declined. Likewise, mid- and small-cap stocks advanced during the quarter and outperformed large-cap stocks. Overall, U.S. stocks trailed non-U.S. developed markets and emerging markets equities.

Consumer discretionary weighed on returns. Overweights to Tesla (TSLA) and DoorDash (DASH) relative to the benchmark were the key detractors. The electric vehicle maker's shares declined after reporting weaker-than-expected deliveries, as investors grew cautious about demand momentum and the near-term growth outlook for the company's core business.

Consumer staples hurt. A lack of exposure to consumer staples, one of the benchmark's top-performing sectors during the period, weighed on relative results.

Information technology helped. Stock selection and a sector underweight relative to the benchmark aided performance, with gains from semiconductor-related holdings. Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) was among the contributors, as the maker of semiconductor-based power solutions rallied after reporting better-than-expected earnings results and guidance that highlighted robust demand from data centers and automotive applications.

Key Contributors

Microsoft (MSFT). Underweight exposure to Microsoft relative to the benchmark boosted returns. The market's focus on the company's cloud computing platform Azure, growth and concerns around rising competition in artificial intelligence (AI) drove shares lower. Because Microsoft doesn't have its own AI model, some investors believe it has exposure to the software industry threat posed by AI.

Ascendis Pharma (ASND). The Denmark-based biotechnology firm's shares continued to outperform the broader market. We believe the stock has benefited from favorable pipeline updates that, combined with recent earnings momentum and increasing global adoption, have driven positive investor sentiment.

Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR). Monolithic is an earlier-stage analog semiconductor manufacturer aiming to reduce energy consumption in customer systems. The company reported better-than-expected earnings results, benefiting from robust demand from AI data centers and automotive applications.

Key Detractors

Adyen (ADYEY). Shares of this Netherlands-based payment solutions provider declined as the company posted slower-than-expected revenue growth. Although forward guidance was revised down, we believe the company's absolute growth rates and valuation remain attractive.

Apple (AAPL). Not owning Apple weighed on relative returns as the stock rallied in January and performed better than the broader market in March. Better-than-expected earnings, resilient services growth and improving sentiment around iPhone demand in key international markets supported the stock.

Costco Wholesale (COST). Our lack of exposure to Costco hurt relative performance. The retailer's stock outperformed amid better-than-expected earnings, which were driven by better-than-expected growth in product sales and membership fees and ongoing customer traffic gains.

Notable Trades

Lam Research (LRCX). Lam makes the tools needed for more complex chips with more processing steps and tightly layered architectures. We believe surging artificial intelligence (AI)-driven demand for wafer fabrication equipment and a widening supply/demand imbalance in memory will likely drive sustained, above-cycle growth for the company.

Cognex (CGNX). Cognex is a specialized machine vision company selling products for manufacturing and industrial 2D and 3D vision systems. As our research has indicated that its barcode identification products are essential to automation and quality assurance at high speed, we believe it's a long-term growth investment with excellent potential in several secular growth areas.

Paylocity (PCTY). We eliminated our stake in payroll software provider Paylocity. The stock has suffered amid the market sell-off of enterprise software companies. We used the proceeds to fund a position in Reddit, which we believe is competitively well positioned.

Figma (FIG). Figma offers collaborative software used for website, app and mobile interface design. Unfortunately, we believe the company has been a victim of market fears around threats to its business from AI. We moved on from the position, choosing to put the capital to work in names where we believe there is greater business clarity and potential.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital appreciation through investments in 30-45 quality, large-cap, U.S. growth oriented companies.

Portfolio Positioning

Our process uses bottom-up financial analysis aimed at identifying large-cap companies that we think have the potential to produce attractive long-term earnings growth. We seek to reduce unintended, nonfinancial risks and align the portfolio with company-specific risks that we believe will be rewarded over time. As a result of this approach, our sector and industry allocations reflect areas of the market where we believe we are finding opportunities at a given time.

Opportunity amid uncertainty. We believe uncertainty has rarely been higher as a war in the Middle East, monetary policy and the global geopolitical order and trade regime are all in flux. To top it off, no one knows with any certainty how profound the economic impacts of artificial intelligence (AI) will ultimately be. These technological, economic and policy uncertainties go a long way to explaining why we expect market volatility to continue in 2026. While we remain mindful of these conditions, they have not been central to our investment thesis. We seek to look beyond economic cycles as we attempt to identify the next decade's potential winners. Indeed, we remain positive on growth stocks over time because we have continued to find what we believe are innovative, secular growth companies producing strong, sustainable earnings growth.

Focus on durable earnings growth. We believe corporate earnings are key because stock returns are often a function of earnings growth and the price investors are willing to pay for those earnings. The good news, in our opinion, is that earnings forecasts for 2026 have been positive. However, we believe the war in Iran will likely mean higher energy prices, which could lead to slower economic growth and higher inflation. However, growth-oriented companies—whose earnings tend to be more durable—are generally more attractive when the economic outlook is uncertain, in our view. Indeed, we have continued to see what we think are tremendous opportunities in companies that have been benefiting from enduring, secular trends such as AI, enterprise digital transformation, cybersecurity, factory automation and drug discovery, regardless of economic and policy outcomes.

Carefully evaluating technology stocks. In our opinion, a key consideration when determining the future market direction is the efficacy and durability of corporate spending on AI. Although we do not subscribe to the argument that AI stocks are in a bubble, we remain diligent in our research as we seek evidence of inflated returns on AI-related capital expenditures. To achieve this, we ask pointed questions of corporate management teams and dig into their financials with a fine-tooth comb. We believe that winners and losers from this massive investment will be differentiated by how effective they have been at deploying AI technology and managing the disruption to their business models. This is why we don't invest based on high-level investment themes but look at individual company results to identify what we see as attractive opportunities.

Volatility presents opportunities. Business conditions generally vary from quarter to quarter and year to year, and stocks can go up or down in the near term for any number of reasons. However, we believe companies with solid long-term growth prospects are typically better situated to ride out uncertainty relating to economic and earnings growth. We believe our portfolio investments present long-term structural growth opportunities. Of course, we acknowledge the human tragedy of war and recognize that it will likely have profound consequences for people, economics and politics going forward. That said, it is our job to remain vigilant in monitoring the financial progress and risks of our investments. As such, we seek to utilize short-term volatility as an opportunity to add to positions when we see share prices disconnect from our assessment of what we believe is their long-term potential.

data.title: Our Long-Term And Short-Term Stock Market Outlook
data.description:
In this piece, we present our long-term and short-term analysis of the stock market.

Long-Term

Both the stock market and the real estate market are cyclical. The major cycle is about 20 years in wavelength and corresponds to the length of a human generation. Bull market phases of ~20 years are separated by periods of range-bound trading that last ~12 years on average. The current bull market is ~16 years old, and, therefore, it can be reasonably expected to have another couple of years of life left in it.

The real estate cycle can peak ahead of the stock market or at about the same time as the stock market. The chart below suggests that the real estate cycle will peak in 2026, but it could easily peak in 2027, and the stock market could peak around the same time.

The cause, or “trigger,” of financial panics/recessions/bear markets has always been unsustainable private debt, not public “debt,” like the mainstream financial commentators are constantly harping about. Public “debt” is the private sector surplus, and if the public “debt” increases, then the private sector surplus also increases. However, when private debt (bank credit) increases to the point where it can no longer be serviced, then assets are sold and recession (deflation) begins.

Currently, private debt is not a problem and has room to grow. The chart below shows that household debt and delinquency rates for consumer and business loans are all near historic lows.

Recessions/bear markets occur 6-18 months after the second 10y-2y Treasury rate inversion. The rate differential has inverted only once (pink oval) and is still positive and rising (purple line). If we get the second inversion late in 2026, then a bear market could happen sometime in 2027 (chart below). If the inversion is delayed further, then the bear market will also be delayed.

No two markets are exactly alike, but the tech bull market of the 1990s is similar in many ways to the current AI bull market: both markets were driven by a disruptive technology; both had money pouring into them (in the 90s it was driven by private debt, but this time there is both private debt and government deficits financing the AI bull); and both markets had the mainstream financial commentators in disbelief that the market could go higher.

The two charts below show the similar patterns in the SPX price, SPX yield, 6-month T-rate, and Bank of America stock price for the 1990-2001 and 2019-2019 periods.

In conclusion, with bank credit still in an expansionary phase, a market top in 2026 is less likely than in 2027-8.

Short-Term Models

Both the reserve and the liquidity models are in headwind periods until the first week in May. This implies weakness in the SPX and a buy-the-dip opportunity.

The liquidity ROC (rate of change) model has made its “x” low and is rising. The SPX is still trying to make its “w” high (a 5-week lag), after which a pullback is expected down to its “w” low, followed by a resumption of the rally to the “x” high.

The repo model has made its #32 high and has been dropping for 2 weeks. The SPX is close to its #32 high, after which a pullback is expected.

The quarterly interest payments are taken from what is called the “other reserves” account. There is a pattern of SPX weakness around these payments. The next payment is due on May 15.

As long as the net money creation continues to be positive, the stock market will be supported, but pullbacks should be expected and used as buying opportunities. Broad-spectrum ETFs such as IWM, DIA, QQQ, and SPY can be bought on pullbacks.

data.title: AMD: Compute-For-Equity Looks Dilutive, But It's Actually A Strategic Masterstroke
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) has gained 62% since my last report and now exceeds my bullish price target of $304.20. In my prior report, I received comments on AMD giving out shares to hyperscalers. While this may seem like AMD is actively buying itself into agreements, I believe that simply viewing this as shareholder dilution with customers receiving free chips is too oversimplified. In this report, I discuss why the compute-for-equity setup makes sense.

AMD Needs To Gain Market Share - But Not At All Costs

Looking at the data center sales for AMD against Nvidia (NVDA), it is quite clear that Nvidia is the undisputed leader. Both companies take a bit of a different approach. Nvidia is big in the combination of GPUs and data links, while AMD bets on the strength of its CPU+GPU setup. We see that Nvidia’s share in data center sales against AMD is over 90%. In order to win market share, AMD could elect to lower its sales prices. That would give AMD a stronger footing with customers. However, the drawback of such a setup is quite simply that AMD would lose all pricing power.

I believe that AMD could get revenues anywhere between $10 billion and $20 billion per GW deployed. The company has a 6 GW agreement with Meta Platforms (META), with 160 million shares vesting based on capacity deployed and share prices. A similar agreement exists with OpenAI. Potentially we are looking at 320 million shares vesting after $120 billion to $240 billion in sales from these two agreements. AMD has around 1,624 million shares outstanding, so the potential dilution is 16.5%. That is obviously substantial, but I believe that if we look at follow-up orders, it is better for AMD and shareholders to stomach a 16.5% dilution rather than having reduced pricing power on future sales agreements with OpenAI and Meta, but also with other companies. Also, if we look at the market shares, AMD needs to do something in order to not fully give the market for general GPUs to Nvidia.

Exact Vesting Terms Are Not Known

What is somewhat unfortunate is that the exact vesting terms for the 160 million warrants are not known. I am currently assuming vesting as shown below, and that is on the assumption that everything vests with the exact vesting terms on price unknown.

More than likely, the vesting conditions for the earliest tranches are less restricted by AMD’s share prices, but the later tranches will have more strict vesting conditions on the share prices.

AMD Stock Has Upside Even With Compute-For-Equity Transactions

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For AMD, we note that the stock is fairly valued against a peer group valuation when looking at the earnings for 2027. However, when considering the median EV/EBITDA for AMD, we note that there still is 64% upside. This is when we model in 50% of the warrants vesting by 2028. My prior price target for AMD was $538.43 for 2027, and it is $569.28 now. So, even with the potential dilution from vesting, the upside has expanded. I would say that also makes sense; the expected growth rates of AMD are 36% on sales, 63% on EBITDA, and 43% on free cash flow. Putting it differently, the company is expected to grow sales, EBITDA, and free cash flow faster than the dilutive impact. That is not to say that AMD would otherwise not have reached a 36% growth rate because I think even without these agreements it would, but it provides AMD with an entry for follow-up orders from Meta and OpenAI without compromising on pricing.

Essentially, if Meta and OpenAI warrants vest and AMD shares hit $600, then the stake of these two companies in AMD would be valued at $192 billion. That 192 billion can then be redeployed to buy additional compute capacity. Some may view it as AMD giving these companies a discount, and in some ways it is. If they buy similar tranches, then another $120-$240 billion could be purchased with the $192 billion that the stake could potentially be worth, but the company is doing that without actually discounting the GPUs, and that is key.

For AMD, the margins are set to increase to 33% by 2028, with free cash flow hitting close to $20 billion on a very manageable CapEx profile. I have modeled the tranches vesting as a share issuance against the current share price, and that is simply to get the share counts right, as I have also provided an offset investment so that there is no dollar value added to the cash pile from the vesting apart from the exercise price. What we do note is that by 2028, 47% of the warrants could potentially be vested. At $600 per share, that is worth $96 billion. AMD’s cash pile is set to rise from around $5-$6 billion in 2023-2024 to $44.5 billion.

Essentially, the company has the space to repurchase $40 billion in shares back. So, the dilution of 16.5% eventually could be brought down substantially to 10% or less. I believe that no matter how you look at it, the growth that AMD will experience will more than offset the dilutive impact that vesting of the warrants could have.

Conclusion: Compute-for-Equity Makes Sense

AMD’s agreement with OpenAI and Meta, including the vesting of potentially up to 320 million shares, may look painful for shareholders. However, I believe that the net impact could be substantially smaller and provide AMD with a stronger entry at hyperscalers without compromising on GPU prices. The agreements include vesting terms in order to not lower pricing power and achieve follow-up orders, which should come at substantially better terms for AMD, and that is something the company would not have if it were to directly apply discounts on its GPU products.

data.title: SES AI Q1 Earnings: Strong Synergies For 2026
data.description:
SES AI (SES) reported in-line Q1-2026 financial results last Thursday. Revenue increased, profit margins improved, and net loss improved. The company announced during Q4 a change in revenue sources, which had investors on edge. Q1 results show strong revenue momentum and should ease their concern.

The stock price is up 33.5% over the last year, although it has dropped from its 2025 highs and even more after the company announced the revenue change. I covered these changes earlier this month on Seeking Alpha. The stock has dropped 2.52% since then. The company has strong potential over 2026 with its ESS sales. Drone battery sales and battery materials sales will also drive growth. I continue my rating of a Hold.

Current Synergies

SES AI has refocused its operating segments into three categories: ESS sales, drone core battery sales, and battery material sales, including their development under Molecular Universe subscriptions. Last year, the company was bringing in most of its revenue from ongoing contracts for its EV battery B samples.

SES AI will enter the North American ESS market with a new multi-year distribution agreement with ATG EPower. SES AI will gain access to ATG EPower’s distribution network, which includes residential, commercial, and industrial clients. SES AI, via UZ Energy, will sell its ESS hardware and battery software through the network.

SES AI has completed the conversion of its South Korean battery manufacturing facility. The NDAA-compliant facility will now manufacture drone batteries. The company aims to ramp up manufacturing capabilities to 1 million drone cells per year. The drone battery cells have already been provided to potential clients, including defense companies and commercial ones. SES AI plans to open another manufacturing facility in Southeast Asia.

SES AI has a group of clients who license its Molecular Universe AI platform. Some of them have progressed to a second phase of testing bespoke battery materials. The next step will be the manufacture of battery materials. The company has a joint venture with Hisun to manufacture these materials.

SES AI released a new version (2.5) of its Molecular Universe AI platform. The system discovers new materials, like electrolytes, for electric batteries. The materials vary depending on the battery needs. SES AI recently gained a new subscriber to its platform. A major global battery manufacturer pledged a multi-year subscription to Molecular Universe. SES AI expects these licenses to bring in revenue over 2026.

Q1-2026 Results

The company reported revenue of $6.7 million, representing a 47% increase QoQ and a 16% increase YoY. Revenue from UZ Energy drove the increases. Gross profit was $1.2 million, a 140% increase QoQ and a 74% decrease YoY. The change QoQ and YoY was due to a different revenue mix. Profit margin was 18.10% versus 11.27% QoQ and 78.66% YoY. The flux was caused by a change in revenue mix. The company reports improvements from its ESS business, its sample batteries for drones, and new Molecular Universe subscriptions.

Net loss was $12.1 million versus a net loss of $17 million QoQ and a net loss of $12.4 million YoY. Net loss will continue until the company increases its quarterly revenue. Cash and ST investments at the end of Q1 were $177.7 million. The company remains debt-free.

SES AI forecasts between $30 million and $35 million in revenue for 2026, mostly from ESS sales. Market consensus for the company’s Q2 revenue is $5.29 million. The company will report in July.

The company has now reported revenue for six quarters. Overall, the amount has increased, and the company expects the trend to continue through 2026. There is still not enough revenue to cover operating costs. This may not change until 2027 or later. The company has a strong balance sheet with no debt. There is plenty of liquidity to expand operations.

As it stands, the company is overvalued. The stock price traded above its book value per share and NAV per share value. Market cap is higher than total EV. Higher revenue will allow for better valuation.

Investment Strategy and Risk

SES AI is at low risk of poor financial performance. Total assets outweigh liabilities. Cash and ST investments allow for continued operations and expansion. The company should increase its revenue through expanded operations in its ESS segment and drone battery sales.

A Hold rating for the company comes as a moderate risk. The stock has not returned to its previous 2025 highs, between $2 and $3 per share. The revenue change has proven to be of no real issue to the company’s fundamentals. Investors have not returned to a strong sentiment yet. The price will likely remain volatile.

Conclusion

SES AI showed impressive results for Q1-2026. The company has increased its revenue, despite an end to its electric battery contracts. The company made up the difference with sales from its ESS segment. There are additional synergies on the horizon for 2026, and the company maintains a positive forward outlook. Its balance sheet is strong, and forward momentum is in place. I continue my rating of a Hold and believe the company has long-term potential.
